figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of MetaColloc. During meta-training (a), coordinate samples x are drawn from multi-scale Gaussian Random Fields (GRF). The dual-branch network processes inputs through a lowfrequency branch (raw coordinates) and a high-frequency branch (multi-scale Fourier embedding), whose outputs are concatenated to form the basis matrix Φ. Network parameters θ are updated via least-squares loss on GRF tasks. At test time (b), θ is frozen; given collocation points xint from a new PDE, the network produces Φ and its derivatives Φx,Φxx, ..., which are assembled into a linear system solved in closed form. The PDE solution is then recovered as u(x) = Φ(x) · w, requiring neither solution data nor gradient-based optimization.
Table 1: RMSE comparison across six PDEs. We report the mean and 95% confidence interval. Lower is better.

Figure 4: Exact solution, MetaColloc prediction, and absolute error heatmap for the Poisson equation on L-shape domain with mixed Dirichlet and Neumann boundaries. The error is well-bounded across the entire domain, including the challenging re-entrant corner of the L-shape.
Table 2: RMSE comparison against baseline methods. We report the mean and 95% confidence interval. Lower is better.

Figure 5: Exact solution, MetaColloc prediction, and absolute error heatmap for the Poisson equation on Annulus domain with mixed Dirichlet and Robin boundaries. The error is well-bounded across the entire domain.

Figure 3: RMSE as a function of the number of iterations K for the Sine-Gordon and KdV equations. We run five seeds for each setting. The curves show stable and smooth decay. The Sine-Gordon equation converges fast. The KdV equation needs more steps but still improves in a steady way.

Figure 2: Frequency sweep exposes the spectral limitations of MetaColloc. Although the metalearned basis can represent high-frequency modes in function space, its Laplacian responses become increasingly unstable as frequency grows. The divergence between RMSE(u) and RMSE(∆u) highlights a structural gap between interpolation accuracy and PDE operator compatibility. This phenomenon reflects a general property of function-only meta-objectives rather than a failure of the architecture, and suggests a natural direction for operator-aware meta-learning.
Table 6: Solving time comparison (mean with 95% CI) across six PDEs. The unit of data is seconds. Lower is better.
Table 3: RMSE comparison (mean with 95% CI) across six PDEs. Lower is better.
